pages.title: Emotion-Aware ASR
pages.kicker: Do not turn affect into a single transcript label

cards.title: Define the construct
cards.body: Choose arousal, valence, frustration, laughter, sentiment, or risk before choosing a model.

cards.title: Pick the time scale
cards.body: Frame, utterance, speaker turn, and conversation labels answer different research questions.

cards.title: Constrain the action
cards.body: Use calibrated affect as evidence for safe behavior, not as a claim about a person.

pages.title: Four Signals, Four Boundaries
pages.kicker: Emotion-aware ASR should keep linguistic, acoustic, speaker, and contextual evidence distinct.

cards.title: Words
cards.body: Lexical content can signal sentiment but misses vocal delivery.

cards.title: Prosody
cards.body: Pitch, energy, rhythm, and pauses carry affective cues.

cards.title: Speaker baseline
cards.body: The same acoustic pattern can mean different things across people.

cards.title: Conversation context
cards.body: Turn history and situation change how an utterance should be interpreted.

pages.title: Emotion Labels Are Noisy
pages.kicker: The hardest errors often begin with ambiguous labels rather than model architecture.

cards.title: Acted emotion
cards.body: Studio performances may exaggerate cues absent from natural conversation.

cards.title: Annotator disagreement
cards.body: Listeners often disagree on mixed, subtle, or culturally specific affect.

cards.title: Domain shift
cards.body: Models trained on one language or channel can fail elsewhere.

cards.title: False certainty
cards.body: A single emotion label can hide uncertainty and multiple plausible readings.
